prompt: Explain photosynthesis to a curious adult non-scientist in around 150 words. Avoid a bulleted list.
response: Photosynthesis is how plants make their own food using sunlight, air, and water. Imagine a plant as a tiny green kitchen. The leaves are the main cooking area, equipped with a special green pigment called chlorophyll that captures sunlight—like solar panels soaking up energy. When sunlight hits the leaves, it powers a chemical reaction that combines carbon dioxide (which the plant pulls in from the air through tiny holes in the leaves) with water (absorbed from the soil through the roots). Together, these ingredients are transformed into glucose, a type of sugar that gives the plant energy to grow. As a side effect, oxygen is released into the air—this is the oxygen we breathe! So, in essence, photosynthesis is nature’s way of converting sunlight into food and life-sustaining oxygen. It’s why plants are so essential—they not only feed themselves but also support nearly all life on Earth by producing the air we need.
